message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The COVID-19 pandemic has resulted in more than 3.5 million cases and
                        245 thousand deaths worldwide as of May 6, 2020. Determining the extent of
                        the presence of the virus on public surfaces is critical for understanding
                        the potential risk of infection in these areas.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the presence of SARS-CoV-2 RNA on public surfaces in a
                        densely populated urban area in Brazil.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design and Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 101 samples were collected from different surfaces in public
                        places in the region of Belo Horizonte with the highest number of COVID-19
                        cases. Samples were collected near the hospital and public transportation
                        areas using sterile swabs, and then submitted to nucleic acid extraction and
                        genomic detection and quantification by one-step qPCR.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Seventeen of the 101 samples tested positive (16.8%) for SARS-CoV-2 RNA,
                        including samples from bus stations/terminals, public squares, and
                        sidewalks, including those near hospitals.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our data indicated the contamination of public surfaces by SARS-CoV-2,
                        especially near hospital areas, highlighting the risk of infection for the
                        population. Constant monitoring of the virus in urban areas is required as a
                        strategy to fight the pandemic and prevent further infections.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:label/>
                  <jats:p>Can SARS-CoV-2 be detected on surfaces in public areas used by a large
                        proportion of the population?</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 RNA was detected in different locations, including bus
                        stations/terminals, squares, and sidewalks, especially those near hospitals,
                        in a densely populated area of Belo Horizonte, Brazil.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study highlights the need for constant monitoring for the presence
                        of SARS-CoV-2 RNA in urban areas to support better strategies to fight the
                        COVID-19 pandemic and prevent further infections.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title:
Detection of SARS-CoV-2 RNA on public surfaces in a densely populated urban
                    area of Brazil

reference.article-title: The species Severe acute respiratory syndrome-related coronavirus: classifying 2019-nCoV and naming it SARS-CoV-2
reference.author: Coronaviridae Study Group of the International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses
